content.text: are two essential data handling abstractions from
that make data loading, batching, and preprocessing easy for training deep learning models.

: A big basket holding all your data (images, labels, text) that gives you one piece at a time when asked
-
: A server that takes the basket and serves data to your model in batches (e.g., 32 images at once) with shuffling and parallel loading
Together, they make training smooth and fast by abstracting data management.
Why They Matter in Generative AI
Training on massive datasets (millions of images for GANs, diffusion)
GPU memory limits require controlled batch sizes
Models need diverse data patterns (not ordered sequences)
Data loading slower than GPU computation
Custom data formats (medical images, art datasets)
They enable efficient data management, batching, shuffling, and parallel loading—essential for training large-scale generative models.
representing your dataset. You use built-in datasets (MNIST, CIFAR) or create your own by extending it.

from torch.utils.data import Dataset

class MyDataset(Dataset):
    def __init__(self):
        # Load data (from files, folders, CSV, etc.)
        self.data = load_my_data()  # [web:31]
        self.labels = load_my_labels()
    
    def __len__(self):
        # Return total number of samples
        return len(self.data)  # [web:31][web:32]
    
    def __getitem__(self, index):
        # Return one sample + label at index
        sample = self.data[index]
        label = self.labels[index]
        return sample, label  # [web:31][web:32]

from torch.utils.data import DataLoader

train_loader = DataLoader(
    dataset=dataset,       # Your Dataset object [web:30]
    batch_size=32,         # Samples per batch [web:32][web:35]
    shuffle=True,          # Shuffle data [web:30][web:35]
    num_workers=2,         # Subprocesses for loading [web:30][web:35]
    pin_memory=True,       # Faster GPU transfer [web:30]
    drop_last=True         # Drop incomplete batch [web:30]
)

# DataLoader calls __getitem__() and wraps for batch [web:33]
for batch_idx, (images, labels) in enumerate(train_loader):
    # images: tensor of shape (batch_size, channels, H, W)
    # labels: tensor of shape (batch_size,)
    
    # Forward pass
    outputs = model(images)
    loss = loss_fn(outputs, labels)
    
    # Backward pass
    loss.backward()
    optimizer.step()
from the Dataset and wraps for the entire batch during training.
Integration with Tensors, Autograd, and Modules

import torch
import torch.nn as nn
from torch.utils.data import Dataset, DataLoader

# 1. Define Dataset
class GANDataset(Dataset):
    def __init__(self, image_path):
        self.images = load_images(image_path)
    
    def __len__(self):
        return len(self.images)
    
    def __getitem__(self, idx):
        image = self.images[idx]
        image = preprocess(image)  # Convert to tensor
        return image

# 2. Define Model (nn.Module)
class Generator(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__()
        self.network = nn.Sequential(
            nn.Linear(128, 256),
            nn.ReLU(),
            nn.Linear(256, 784)
        )
    
    def forward(self, x):
        return self.network(x)

# 3. Create DataLoader
dataset = GANDataset("/path/to/training-images")
train_loader = DataLoader(
    dataset,
    batch_size=64,
    shuffle=True,
    num_workers=4
)

# 4. Training loop (Tensors + Autograd)
model = Generator()
optimizer = torch.optim.Adam(model.parameters(), lr=0.001)

for epoch in range(100):
    for batch_idx, real_images in enumerate(train_loader):
        # real_images is a tensor (64, 3, 256, 256)
        
        # Generate fake images
        noise = torch.randn(64, 128)
        fake_images = model(noise)
        
        # Compute loss (Tensor operation)
        loss = compute_gan_loss(fake_images, real_images)
        
        # Backpropagate (Autograd computes gradients)
        loss.backward()  # [web:1]
        optimizer.step()

from torchvision.datasets import MNIST, CIFAR10, FashionMNIST
from torch.utils.data import DataLoader

# MNIST (for simple GAN demos)
mnist = MNIST(root="./data", download=True, train=True)
train_loader = DataLoader(mnist, batch_size=32, shuffle=True)

# CIFAR-10 (for more complex generative models)
cifar = CIFAR10(root="./data", download=True, train=True)
train_loader = DataLoader(cifar, batch_size=64, shuffle=True, num_workers=4)

# ImageFolder (generic for custom image datasets)
from torchvision.datasets import ImageFolder
dataset = ImageFolder(root="/path/to/images")
loader = DataLoader(dataset, batch_size=32, shuffle=True)
Think of Dataset + DataLoader as a
: Contains all raw ingredients (images), organized with labels
: Chef fetches one ingredient at a specific location
: Takes ingredients from storage and serves them in batches to the cook
: Cook gets 32 plates at once (not one by one)
: Randomize order so cook doesn't memorize sequences
: Processes batches to create the dish (training)
This abstraction lets you focus on model design, not data handling.
PyTorch's Dataset and DataLoader abstractions provide concrete advantages over rolling your own data pipeline from scratch:
Zero GPU idle time with prefetching:

Setting num_workers > 0 launches background processes that load and preprocess the next batch while the GPU trains on the current one. Without prefetching, the GPU waits for data on every step, wasting compute.

DataLoader stacks individual samples returned by __getitem__() into a batched tensor automatically. The default collate_fn handles numpy arrays, PIL Images, and Python scalars with no manual stacking.
Passing generator=torch.Generator().manual_seed(42) gives deterministic shuffle order across runs, making experiments reproducible without sacrificing randomness.
Transforms inside __getitem__() run in worker processes, not the main process. CPU preprocessing is truly parallelised and does not block the training loop.
The streaming variant loads data from disk or network one sample at a time, enabling training on datasets that do not fit in RAM—essential for LLM pretraining on terabyte corpora.
DistributedSampler partitions the dataset across GPUs with no changes to the Dataset class. Combined with torch.nn.parallel.DistributedDataParallel, the same pipeline scales from 1 GPU to hundreds.

On Windows, multiprocessing uses spawn (not fork), so each worker re-imports the full module. This adds seconds of startup latency and requires wrapping training in if __name__ == '__main__':. On Linux, fork-based spawning is near-zero cost.
pin_memory=True allocates tensors in CUDA-pinned (non-pageable) RAM, which speeds GPU transfers but is a limited OS resource. Allocating too much pinned memory degrades overall system performance.
Variable-length sequences require a custom collate_fn:
The default collate_fn cannot batch tensors of different shapes. Text or audio datasets almost always need a custom function that pads sequences to a common length within each batch.
Random access requirement for map-style datasets:
Map-style Dataset assumes O(1) index access. If your data source (compressed archive, database query) has linear access cost, build an index or switch to IterableDataset.
The optimal number of workers depends on CPU cores, disk speed, and preprocessing cost. Too many workers can saturate disk I/O and slow everything down. Rule of thumb: start at 2–4 and benchmark.
Use Dataset and DataLoader whenever you train a PyTorch model and load data from files, databases, or APIs. The abstraction pays off even for small datasets because it standardises the training loop.
Training image generative models (GANs, diffusion) on large image folders — use ImageFolder or a custom Dataset with torchvision transforms.
Fine-tuning language models on a custom corpus — use IterableDataset reading from pre-tokenised JSONL files on disk or from HuggingFace Hub.
Multi-modal training with image+text pairs — return a dict from __getitem__() and write a collate_fn that pads text to the batch's maximum length.
Distributed training across multiple GPUs using DistributedSampler and DDP.
The entire dataset fits in GPU VRAM — load once as a tensor and iterate manually; DataLoader's per-batch overhead is unnecessary overhead in that case.

Online reinforcement learning where samples are generated by the agent's own actions — a replay buffer with custom sampling (prioritised experience replay) fits better than the standard DataLoader contract.
You are using a non-PyTorch framework (TensorFlow tf.data, JAX dataloaders) — the abstractions do not transfer across frameworks.
These mistakes appear repeatedly and cause silent data pipeline bugs or severe performance regressions.
Mistake 1 — Loading all data into RAM in __init__
Mistake 2 — Storing non-picklable objects on the Dataset
Mistake 3 — Using shuffle=True on an IterableDataset

# WRONG: raises TypeError — IterableDataset has no index for shuffling
loader = DataLoader(iterable_dataset, batch_size=32, shuffle=True)  # ERROR

# RIGHT: implement shuffle inside the IterableDataset via a buffer
import random
from torch.utils.data import IterableDataset

class BufferedShuffleDataset(IterableDataset):
    def __init__(self, source, buffer_size=1000):
        self.source = source
        self.buffer_size = buffer_size

    def __iter__(self):
        buf = []
        for sample in self.source:
            buf.append(sample)
            if len(buf) >= self.buffer_size:
                random.shuffle(buf)
                yield from buf
                buf = []
        random.shuffle(buf)
        yield from buf

loader = DataLoader(BufferedShuffleDataset(raw_dataset), batch_size=32)  # OK
Set worker_init_fn to fix random seeds in workers.
Without it, every worker starts with the same numpy/Python random seed, producing correlated augmentation across workers. Use: worker_init_fn=lambda wid: np.random.seed(torch.initial_seed() % 2**32).
Use drop_last=True during training for consistent batch sizes.
A partial final batch causes BatchNorm statistics to be unreliable and can crash code that assumes a fixed batch size. Set drop_last=False only for evaluation.
Profile the pipeline before tuning the model.
If GPU utilisation in nvidia-smi is below 80%, data loading is the bottleneck, not the model. Increase num_workers, add pin_memory=True, or cache preprocessed data.
For LLM fine-tuning, tokenisation is expensive. Run it once offline, save to disk with np.memmap or datasets.save_to_disk(), and load cached tokens in __getitem__.
Use persistent_workers=True for small datasets with num_workers > 0.
By default, workers are killed and respawned between epochs. persistent_workers=True keeps them alive and saves the spawn cost—critical when epoch time is short.
Keep train and val loaders separate.
Train loader: shuffle=True, augmentation transforms. Val loader: shuffle=False, normalisation transforms only. Never share a single DataLoader for both modes.

Data loading easily becomes the training bottleneck, especially with heavy augmentation or slow storage. The table below shows the impact of key parameters on an image training task (100K 256x256 images, A100 GPU):

For very large-scale training (petabyte datasets), WebDataset stores data in tar shards and streams from cloud storage while keeping the DataLoader interface. This avoids local-disk bottlenecks entirely.

import torch, time
from torch.utils.data import DataLoader, TensorDataset

# Benchmark different num_workers settings
dataset = TensorDataset(torch.randn(10_000, 3, 224, 224))

for nw in [0, 2, 4, 8]:
    loader = DataLoader(dataset, batch_size=64, num_workers=nw, pin_memory=True)
    start = time.perf_counter()
    for batch in loader:
        pass  # simulate GPU step with no-op
    elapsed = time.perf_counter() - start
    print(f"num_workers={nw}: {10_000/elapsed:.0f} samples/sec")
PyTorch Dataset/DataLoader is one of several data loading solutions. The right choice depends on your framework, data format, and scale:
Flexible, native tensor integration, distributed training support
Windows multiprocessing overhead; variable-length sequences need custom collate_fn
Declarative pipeline, native TFRecord support, XLA fusion
TF-only; eager debugging is harder than PyTorch
1-line access to 10K+ NLP datasets, Arrow-backed memory mapping
Optimised for NLP; less convenient for raw image data
Streams tar shards from S3/GCS; petabyte-scale training
Requires data pre-packed as tars; less flexible for random access
GPU-accelerated image decode and augmentation; maximum throughput
NVIDIA GPUs only; steeper learning curve; limited transform library
abstracts your data (X and y) into an object with
wraps Dataset to serve data in mini-batches with shuffling and parallel loading
: Controls how many samples per batch (critical for GPU memory)
: Randomizes data order for better training
: Enables multiprocessing for faster data loading
: Apply preprocessing (resize, flip, color jitter) on-the-fly
: Efficient loading of large image/text datasets for GANs, diffusion, LLMs
to get your data, DataLoader helps you
—Together they make training smooth and fast for any deep learning project.
